1196	\begin{lemma}[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1197	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1198	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1199	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1200	  % LEAN DEPS: the localisation-transport primitives the Lean proof consumes are
1201	  %   IsLocalization.surj                          (clear the denominator of h over A_g)
1202	  %   IsLocalization.map_units                     (the cleared denominator s ∈ powers g is a unit)
1203	  %   IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated        (key ring identification: Away(g·a) on A_h)
1204	  %   IsLocalization.algEquiv                      (ring iso Away(g·a) ≅ A_h)
1205	  %   Module.Free.of_ringEquiv                     (transport freeness across that ring iso)
1206	  %   IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv                (the σ-semilinear module equivalence)
1207	  %   LinearEquiv.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization    (upgrade the R-linear equiv to Away(g·a)-linear)
1208	  % The one primitive NOT packaged by Mathlib — that the composite localisation map
1209	  %   ψ := mkLinearMap(powers h) ∘ mkLinearMap(powers g) : T → (T_g)_h
1210	  % satisfies IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ — is proved directly from the three
1211	  % IsLocalizedModule axioms (units act invertibly, surjectivity up to denominators, the
1212	  % equality criterion); there is no packaged localisation-of-localisation lemma to invoke.
1213	  % NOTE: the closed Lean proof DID find a packaged route — the composite-localisation
1214	  % `IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ` step goes through `IsBaseChange.comp` via
1215	  % `isLocalizedModule_iff_isBaseChange`, so the "no packaged lemma" remark above is superseded.
1216	  % WITNESS: the conclusion holds at the SINGLE product f := g·a (not at a square f²).
1217	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1218	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1219	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1220	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1221	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1222	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1223	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1224	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1225	\end{lemma}
1226	
1227	\begin{proof}
1228	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1229	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1230	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1231	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1232	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1233	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1234	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1235	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1236	
1237	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1238	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1239	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1240	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1241	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1242	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1243	
1244	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1245	  \[
1246	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1247	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1248	  \]
1249	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1250	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1251	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1252	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1253	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1254	
1255	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1256	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1257	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1258	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1259	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1260	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1261	\end{proof}
1262	
1263	\begin{lemma}[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1264	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1265	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1266	  % NOTE (signature): the Lean decl uses a SHARED universe
1267	  %   `(A N : Type u)` (not `(A : Type*) (N : Type*)`). This is load-bearing, not a
1268	  %   convenience: the base-domain-generalizing strong induction applies its IH at
1269	  %   `A_g = Localization.Away g`, and `LocalizedModule S M : Type (max u_R u_M)`, so the
1270	  %   reindexed module escapes `N`'s universe unless `A` and `N` share one. The geometric
1271	  %   target lives entirely in `Type u` (`Scheme.{u}`), so this is the natural setting.
1272	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1273	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1274	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1275	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1276	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1277	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1278	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1279	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1280	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1281	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1282	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1283	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1284	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1285	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1286	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1287	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1288	  core of the induction).}
1289	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1290	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1291	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1292	
1293	\end{lemma}
1294	
1295	\begin{proof}
1296	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1297	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1298	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1299	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1300	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1301	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE: the induction must generalise the base domain A
1302	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
1303	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
1304	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
1305	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
1306	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
1307	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
1308	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
1309	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
1310	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
1311	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
1312	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
1313	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
1314	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
1315	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
1316	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1317	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1318	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1319	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1320	
1321	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1322	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1323	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1324	
1325	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1326	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1327	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1328	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1329	  \begin{enumerate}
1330	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1331	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1332	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1333	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1334	      \(P_d\)-modules
1335	      \[
1336	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1337	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1338	      \]
1339	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1340	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1341	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1342	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1343	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1344	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1345	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1346	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1347	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1348	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1349	      to \(A_g\).
1350	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1351	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1352	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1353	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1354	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1355	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1356	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1357	      over \(A_f\).
1358	      % NOTE: the tower-descent helper lem:gf_away_tower_descent
1359	      % (free_localizationAway_of_away_tower) is now CLOSED and axiom-clean. The L5 sorry that
1360	      % remains is NOT a missing math step — it is a Lean elaboration blocker: the
1361	      % `OreLocalization` instance *presentations* on the localised quotient `(N ⧸ range φ)_g`
1362	      % (CommSemiring / AddCommMonoid / Module-SMul) emitted by `gf_torsion_reindex` are defeq
1363	      % but not instance-transparent-equal to the presentations the helper input expects. The
1364	      % clean fix is non-local: make `gf_torsion_reindex` emit its conclusion over the canonical
1365	      % `OreLocalization.*` instances (its final `exact` / existential binders), after which the
1366	      % recorded five-line assembly closes verbatim. Do NOT re-dispatch a raw L5 prover round
1367	      % without that instance-alignment refactor first.
1368	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1369	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1370	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1371	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1372	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1373	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1374	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1375	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1376	      completes the induction.
1377	  \end{enumerate}
1378	\end{proof}

1380	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
1381	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
1382	
1383	The following two lemmas package the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms
1384	(flatness and module-finiteness) used by the dévissage chain.
1385	
1386	\begin{lemma}
1387	
1388	
1389	[Flat localization away, finite module]
1390	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
1391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
1392	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1393	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
1394	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is flat over \(A_f\).
1395	\end{lemma}
1396	\begin{proof}
1397	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1398	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); free modules are flat.
1399	\end{proof}
1400	
1401	\begin{lemma}
1402	
1403	
1404	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
1405	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
1406	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
1407	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1408	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a
1409	  finite \(B\)-module with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower
1410	  \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\)
1411	  is free over \(A_f\).
1412	\end{lemma}
1413	\begin{proof}
1414	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1415	  Since \(B\) is module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\),
1416	  transitivity of module-finiteness makes \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module.
1417	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} then gives the required \(f\).
1418	\end{proof}

1420	
1421	\begin{theorem}
1422	
1423	
1424	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
1425	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
1426	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1427	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
1428	  % NOTE (re-signed): the Lean signature `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness`
1429	  % previously took `F : X.Modules` with NO coherence hypothesis, under which the
1430	  % statement is FALSE (a non-coherent module sheaf need not become flat on any
1431	  % non-empty open). The decl (not in archon-protected.yaml) was re-signed to carry
1432	  % the coherence encoding below; the Lean file is the source of truth for the exact
1433	  % binders and the full flatness conclusion.
1434	  %
1435	  % Mathlib has no single `IsCoherent` predicate at this pin; coherence over a
1436	  % locally noetherian base is encoded as the conjunction of two existing
1437	  % SheafOfModules predicates on `F`:
1438	  %     [F.IsQuasicoherent]   (SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent,
1439	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent)
1440	  %     [F.IsFiniteType]      (SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType,
1441	  %                            Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Generators)
1442	  %
1443	  % LEAN SIGNATURE HEADER (now landed in the .lean file):
1444	  %   theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
1445	  %       (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] (F : X.Modules)
1446	  %       [F.IsQuasicoherent] [F.IsFiniteType] :
1447	  %       ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧ (the fibrewise flatness conclusion,
1448	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
1449	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
1450	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).
1451	
1452	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1453	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1454	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1455	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1456	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1457	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1458	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1459	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1460	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1461	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1462	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1463	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1464	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1465	
1466	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1467	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1468	\end{theorem}
1469	
1470	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1471	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1472	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1473	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1474	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1475	% by restricting to a non-empty
1476	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1477	\begin{proof}
1478	  
1479	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1480	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1481	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1482	
1483	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is locally of finite
1484	  type it is in particular quasi-compact, so the open subscheme \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\)
1485	  is quasi-compact; choose a finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of
1486	  \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each
1487	  ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1488	
1489	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1490	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1491	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-type hypothesis makes \(M_j\)
1492	  a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\), \(M_j\) is
1493	  the data fed to the algebraic form.
1494	
1495	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1496	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1497	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1498	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1499	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1500	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1501	
1502	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1503	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the fibrewise flatness conclusion then follows from
1504	  these local freeness statements by the criterion that a module flat at every maximal
1505	  ideal is flat. This produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1506	\end{proof}

1508	\section{Mathlib status}
1509	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
1510	
1511	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
1512	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
1513	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
1514	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
1515	generic-flatness argument consumes
1516	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
1517	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
1518	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
1519	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
1520	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
1521	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1522	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
1523	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
1524	are introduced in
1525	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.

1947	  %      `Scheme.Modules.conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv` identifies

1980	\begin{proof}
1981	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs, lem:base_change_mate_unit_value,
1982	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor,
1983	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp,
1984	        def:base_change_mate_inner_value}
1985	  The concrete identity is the instantiation of the abstract variable-legs reindex
1986	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs} at the literal pullback projection legs
1987	  \(g' = \operatorname{pr}_1\), \(f' = \operatorname{pr}_2\): with the codomain read
1988	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} being definitionally the variable-legs read at
1989	  those legs, the concrete statement follows directly. The remainder of this proof records the
1990	  mathematical content of that abstract identity.
1991	
1992	  Throughout, let \(e : X' \xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Spec}(A \otimes_R R')\) be the
1993	  canonical isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackSpecIso_mathlib}, and
1994	  \(\iota_A : A \to A \otimes_R R'\), \(\iota_{R'} : R' \to A \otimes_R R'\) the tensor
1995	  inclusions, so that by Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor} the two projection legs
1996	  factor as
1997	  \[
1998	    g' \;=\; e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A,
1999	    \qquad
2000	    f' \;=\; e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_{R'} .
2001	  \]
2002	  The inner composite \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) is the four-factor map
2003	  \[
2004	    f_{*}\bigl(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\bigr) \;\circ\;
2005	    (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g', f})^{\mathrm{hom}} \;\circ\;
2006	    (\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w)^{\mathrm{hom}} \;\circ\;
2007	    (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f', g})^{\mathrm{inv}},
2008	  \]
2009	  with \(f = \operatorname{Spec}\varphi\), \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\),
2010	  \(\eta^{g'}\) the unit of the \((g')\)-adjunction, and
2011	  \(w : g' \circ f = f' \circ g\) the commutativity of the square.
2012	
2013	  \emph{Why a direct substitution fails.} The identity cannot be obtained by substituting the
2014	  leg factorizations directly, because the legs \(g'\) and \(f'\) appear in multiple positions
2015	  simultaneously: as the index of the adjunction \(\eta^{g'}\), inside the equality proof
2016	  \(w\) parametrizing \(\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w\), inside the argument
2017	  \(f'_{*}\bigl((g')^{*}\widetilde M\bigr)\) of the codomain comparison
2018	  \(\gamma_{\psi}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}), and inside the opaque codomain
2019	  identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}} = \) Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}.
2020	  The reindex is therefore restructured into three sub-steps so that the legs become explicit
2021	  free variables; it is \emph{not} a single rewrite.
2022	
2023	  \emph{(i) Abstract variable-legs restatement.} Restate the codomain read — and, with it, the
2024	  whole four-factor chain — for \emph{generic} morphisms \(g', f'\) carrying the cone-leg
2025	  equalities \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\) and
2026	  \(f' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_{R'}\) as explicit hypotheses, in place of the literal
2027	  projections \(\operatorname{pr}_1, \operatorname{pr}_2\). In this abstract form the legs are
2028	  free variables, not terms pinned by the pullback's universal property, so each can be
2029	  eliminated by substituting its defining equation: the equality proof \(w\), the adjunction
2030	  index, and the \(\gamma_{\psi}\)/\(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) arguments are all expressed in the
2031	  free leg variables and transform uniformly under the substitution. This restatement converts
2032	  the blocked rewrite into a legitimate one; it is the structural heart of the step.
2033	
2034	  \emph{(ii) \(\Gamma\)-collapse of the transparent coherences.} On global sections, read through
2035	  the \(\Gamma\)-pushforward dictionaries (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso},
2036	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}), the two pushforward-composition factors and the
2037	  congruence factor are transparent. The composition coherences collapse to the identity —
2038	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) and
2039	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f',g})^{\mathrm{inv}}\) evaluate, on the top open, to the identity
2040	  map on restriction of scalars (their section-level values are the identity) — and they
2041	  serve only to rebracket the iterated pushforward along the two factorizations
2042	  \((g' \circ f)_* = (f' \circ g)_*\) of the square's composite. (The
2043	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) collapse is a transparent re-association
2044	  that is most naturally accounted for together with the leg-reindex of step~(iii) rather than at
2045	  this point; the \(\Gamma\)-collapse identity itself is the same.) The congruence factor collapses
2046	  to a canonical transport along the equality of opens: its section-level value
2047	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w)^{\mathrm{hom}}\) is the
2048	  presheaf-restriction along the canonical equality of opens, i.e.\ a repackaging along
2049	  that equality carrying no substantive content. Thus after the \(\Gamma\)-collapse the only
2050	  surviving non-trivial factor is \(f_{*}\bigl(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\bigr)\) read on global
2051	  sections.
2052	
2053	  \emph{(iii) Reduction to Seam~1 via the leg-reindex engine.} With the legs substituted
2054	  (step~(i)) to \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), the leg-reindex engine
2055	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}, instantiated at \(a = e\),
2056	  \(b = \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), \(N = \widetilde M\), rewrites the unit factor
2057	  \(\eta^{g'} = \eta^{e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}\), together with the
2058	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})\) coherence, into the affine
2059	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit followed by \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}\) of the
2060	  \(e\)-unit. Since \(e\) is an isomorphism of schemes, its pullback functor is an equivalence
2061	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}) and the \(e\)-unit is invertible, hence an
2062	  isomorphism absorbed into the codomain identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\). The surviving
2063	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit has section value over \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) equal to
2064	  the algebraic unit \(\eta_M : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) of Seam~1
2065	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}). Composing the surviving factor with the
2066	  codomain identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)
2067	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) and reading over \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) by
2068	  restriction of scalars along \(\psi\) identifies all four factors with the restriction of
2069	  scalars along \(\psi\) of the codomain-read transport of the unit value, namely
2070	  \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\)
2071	  (Definition~\ref{def:base_change_mate_inner_value}) — the
2072	  ``\(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), \(\mathcal F'\) is
2073	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\)'' bookkeeping of the source. Each of (i)--(iii) extracts as a
2074	  named lemma; together they close the reindex without ever rewriting the bare legs.
2075	\end{proof}

2132	\begin{proof}
2133	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex, lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
2134	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read, lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv,
2135	        lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso,
2136	        lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}
2137	  Seam~3 is the pullback-side companion of Seam~2: where Seam~2 reads the inner composite
2138	  \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) over \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), here we read its
2139	  \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-transpose over \(\operatorname{Spec} R'\), and the result is the
2140	  extension of scalars along \(\psi\) of the Seam~2 value \(\rho\).
2141	
2142	  \emph{Counit factorization.} The base-change map
2143	  (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is the adjunction transpose of
2144	  \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) under \((g^* \dashv g_*)\) with \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\). By the
2145	  counit form of the hom-set adjunction (\(\operatorname{homEquiv}\)-counit identity for the
2146	  transpose), it factors as \(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by the counit
2147	  \(\varepsilon_g\), with no opaque transpose remaining:
2148	  \(\Gamma(\theta) = \Gamma\bigl(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\bigr) \circ \Gamma(\varepsilon_g)\).
2149	
2150	  \emph{The concrete counit coherence — why it is not a bare computation.} Conjugating
2151	  \(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by \(\varepsilon_g\) by the domain identification
2152	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}) and the codomain
2153	  identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read})
2154	  requires the pullback dictionary \(\Pi_{\psi}\) of
2155	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} — which reads \(g^* = (\operatorname{Spec}\psi)^*\) of a
2156	  tilde as extension of scalars along \(\psi\) — to interact with the geometric counit
2157	  \(\varepsilon_g\). This interaction is \emph{not} a definitional reduction: \(\Pi_{\psi}\) is
2158	  built abstractly as the uniqueness-of-left-adjoints comparison, evaluated from the
2159	  natural-isomorphism of right adjoints \(\gamma_{\psi}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardNatIso})
2160	  via the conjugate-of-isomorphisms equivalence; concretely \(\Pi_{\psi}\) is the component at the
2161	  module of
2162	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{conjIso}(\mathrm{adj}_L, \mathrm{adj}_R)^{-1}(\gamma_{\psi})\bigr)^{-1}\),
2163	  where \(\mathrm{adj}_L = (\widetilde{(-)}_R \dashv \Gamma_R) . (g^* \dashv g_*)\) is the
2164	  composite ``tilde then pullback'' adjunction and
2165	  \(\mathrm{adj}_R = (\,{}^{\psi}\!(-) \dashv {}_{\psi}(-)) . (\widetilde{(-)}_{R'} \dashv
2166	  \Gamma_{R'})\) is the composite ``extension of scalars then tilde'' adjunction. Because the
2167	  dictionary is opaque in this way, the counit cannot be simplified against it by reflexivity
2168	  or by definitional unfolding; the identification must be made through the abstract conjugate calculus.
2169	
2170	  \emph{Conjugation via the composed-adjunction counit-triangle identity.} The required identity
2171	  is the counit-triangle (zig-zag) coherence for the \emph{composed} adjunction
2172	  \((\widetilde{(-)}_{R'} \dashv \Gamma_{R'}) . (g^* \dashv g_*)\), the same composite adjunction
2173	  whose left adjoint \(\Pi_{\psi}\) compares. Read concretely, this coherence states that the
2174	  dictionary \(\Pi_{\psi}\), composed with the geometric counit \(\varepsilon_g\) and the
2175	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit, equals the algebraic counit of the
2176	  extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction \(({}^{\psi}\!(-) \dashv {}_{\psi}(-))\) along
2177	  \(\psi\); equivalently, conjugating \(\Gamma(\varepsilon_g)\) by the tilde dictionaries
2178	  identifies it with the module-level adjunction counit of
2179	  the extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction along \(\psi\). This is the dual companion of the
2180	  unit-across-conjugate coherence (Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}) used in Seam~1 and
2181	  Seam~2. Applying it, conjugation replaces the transpose by extension of scalars along \(\psi\)
2182	  applied to the \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)-section reading \(\rho\) of \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\)
2183	  from Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex} (Seam~2): the geometric counit collapses to
2184	  the module-level counit, and the residual \(g^*\)-leg becomes \({}^{\psi}\!(-) = \) extension of
2185	  scalars along \(\psi\) applied to \(\rho\).
2186	
2187	  \emph{Identification on generators.} Since \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\), its
2188	  extension of scalars along \(\psi\) sends
2189	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) = (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). This
2190	  is precisely the inverse of the regrouping isomorphism
2191	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}) on generators, and both maps are
2192	  \(R'\)-linear, so they coincide.
2193	\end{proof}

2583	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
2584	
2585	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
2586	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
2587	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.
2588	
2589	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
2590	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
2591	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
2592	  \mathlibok
2593	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
2594	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
2595	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
2596	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
2597	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
2598	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
2599	  \[
2600	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
2601	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2602	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
2603	  \]
2604	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
2605	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
2606	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
2607	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
2608	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that

2648	  \[
2649	    \begin{array}{ccc}
2650	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
2651	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]

2655	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
2656	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
2657	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
2658	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
2659	  \[
2660	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2661	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
2662	  \]

2664	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
2665	  \[

2667	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)

2670	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
2671	  \(\mathcal{F}\).

2674	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of

2677	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be

2748	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
2749	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
2750	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
2751	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
2752	  sequence.
2753	
2754	  \medskip
2755	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
2756	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
2757	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
2758	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
2759	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
2760	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
2761	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
2762	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
2763	  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
2764	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
2765	  \[
2766	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
2767	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
2768	  \]
2769	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
2770	
2771	  \medskip
2772	  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
2773	  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
2774	  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
2775	  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
2776	  \[
2777	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2778	    \;=\;
2779	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
2780	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
2781	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
2782	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
2783	    \Bigr),
2784	    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
2785	  \]
2786	  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
2787	  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
2788	  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
2789	  higher cohomology.
2790	
2791	  \medskip
2792	  \emph{The separated case.} Suppose first that \(X\) is separated over \(A\). Then
2793	  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
2794	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
2795	  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
2796	  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
2797	  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
2798	  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
2799	  \[
2800	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2801	    \qquad
2802	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
2803	  \]
2804	  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
2805	  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
2806	  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
2807	  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
2808	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}):
2809	  \[
2810	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
2811	    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
2812	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2813	    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
2814	    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
2815	  \]
2816	  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.
2817	
2818	  \medskip
2819	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated. We
2820	  argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of members of a finite affine
2821	  cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\), each step reducing to the
2822	  separated/affine case. For \(t = 1\), \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the
2823	  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
2824	  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
2825	  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
2826	  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
2827	  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
2828	  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
2829	  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
2830	  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
2831	  \[
2832	    0 \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
2833	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
2834	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),
2835	  \]
2836	  and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness of \(A \to B\) makes
2837	  \(- \otimes_A B\) preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
2838	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
2839	  \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t - 1\) members), and for
2840	  \(W \cap V\) (separated), it follows for \(X\). In degree \(0\) this gives
2841	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as required.
2842	\end{proof}

5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43

46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
