755	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
756	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
757	
758	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
759	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
760	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
761	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
762	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
763	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
764	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
765	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
766	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
767	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
768	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
769	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
770	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
771	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
772	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
773	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
774	
775	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
776	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
777	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
778	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
779	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
780	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
781	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
782	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
783	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
784	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
785	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
786	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
787	
788	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
789	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
790	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
791	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
792	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
793	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
794	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
795	\(x \in R_1\).
796	
797	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
798	
799	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
800	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
801	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
802	  \mathlibok
803	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
804	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
805	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
806	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
807	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
808	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
809	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
810	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
811	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
812	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
813	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).
814	\end{lemma}
815	
816	\begin{lemma}[A span of homogeneous elements is homogeneous]
817	  \label{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}
818	  \lean{Ideal.homogeneous_span}
819	  \mathlibok
820	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
821	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Ideal}).}
822	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(s \subseteq R\) a set each of
823	  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
824	  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
825	  is homogeneous, hence \(xM\) and the auxiliary submodule \(N' + x \cdot N\) are
826	  homogeneous; this homogeneity feeds the kernel/cokernel closure
827	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
828	\end{lemma}
829	
830	\begin{lemma}[Rank--nullity]
831	  \label{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}
832	  \lean{LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker}
833	  \mathlibok
834	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
835	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.FiniteDimensional.Lemmas}).}
836	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
837	  \(\varphi : V \to W\) a \(\kappa\)-linear map. Then
838	  \[
839	    \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
840	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
841	  \]
842	  This is the rank--nullity identity underlying the degreewise difference identity
843	  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}).
844	\end{lemma}
845	
846	\begin{lemma}[Finite generation transfers along a surjection]
847	  \label{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
848	  \lean{Submodule.FG.restrictScalars_of_surjective}
849	  \mathlibok
850	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic}).}
851	  Let \(\varphi : R \to A\) be a surjective ring homomorphism and \(M\) an
852	  \(A\)-module that is finitely generated when viewed as an \(R\)-module via
853	  \(\varphi\). Then \(M\) is finitely generated as an \(A\)-module. Applied to the
854	  surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots,
855	  t_{r-2}]\) killing the endomorphism \(x = t_{r-1}\) that annihilates the two
856	  subquotients, this transfers their finite generation down one generator
857	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
858	\end{lemma}
859	
860	\begin{lemma}[Multiplication by a homogeneous element shifts degree]
861	  \label{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
862	  \lean{SetLike.IsHomogeneousElem.graded_smul}
863	  \mathlibok
864	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GradedMulAction}).}
865	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a graded family acting on a graded family \(\mathcal{M}\)
866	  via a graded scalar multiplication. If \(a \in R\) is homogeneous of degree
867	  \(i\) -- in particular \(x \in R_1\) -- then for \(m \in M_n\) one has
868	  \(a \cdot m \in M_{i+n}\). This is the degree-shifting property that makes \(xM\)
869	  a homogeneous submodule (with degree-\((n+1)\) part \(x \cdot M_n\)) and that
870	  underlies the ambient identity \(x \cdot N \cap M_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap M_n)\)

872	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}).

875	\begin{lemma}[Commutative subalgebra generated by commuting elements]
876	  \label{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
877	  \lean{Algebra.isMulCommutative_adjoin}
878	  \mathlibok
879	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
880	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Algebra.Subalgebra.Lattice}).}
881	  Let \(R\) be a commutative semiring and \(A\) an \(R\)-algebra, possibly
882	  noncommutative. If \(s \subseteq A\) is a set of pairwise-commuting elements
883	  (\(a b = b a\) for all \(a, b \in s\)), then the \(R\)-subalgebra
884	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_R(s)\) generated by \(s\) has commutative multiplication; the
885	  commutative-ring structure on it follows. Applied to the \(r\) pairwise-commuting degree-raising endomorphisms
886	  \(x_0, \dots, x_{r-1} \in \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\), it yields a
887	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots,
888	  x_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); the polynomial action of
889	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) on \(M\) factors through this commutative \(A\)
890	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
891	\end{lemma}

893	\subsubsection*{The internal grading of a homogeneous submodule (split $G_1$)}
894	
895	\begin{lemma}\leanok
896	[$G_1$ (independence) -- the family $n \mapsto M_n \cap p$ is independent]
897	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}
898	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep}
899	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
900	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and let
901	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
902	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
903	  \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces of \(M\) is independent: for each
904	  \(n\),
905	  \[
906	    (M_n \cap p) \;\cap\; \Bigl(\bigsqcup_{m \ne n} (M_m \cap p)\Bigr) \;=\; 0 .
907	  \]
908	  The whole statement lives in the ambient module \(M\): the subspaces
909	  \(M_n \cap p\) are submodules of \(M\), never of a derived carrier \(\smash{\hat p}\).
910	\end{lemma}
911	
912	\begin{proof}
913	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
914	  The components \(M_n\) of the internal decomposition of \(M\) are independent. The
915	  subfamily \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) consists of submodules with \(M_n \cap p \le M_n\),
916	  so independence is inherited: a relation among the \(M_n \cap p\) is in particular a
917	  relation among the \(M_n\), forcing each term to vanish.
918	\end{proof}
919	
920	\begin{lemma}\leanok
921	[$G_1$ (supremum) -- $\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) = p$ for $p$ homogeneous]
922	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
923	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_inf_eq}
924	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
925	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
926	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then
927	  \[
928	    \bigsqcup_{n} (M_n \cap p) \;=\; p .
929	  \]
930	  Together with the independence of
931	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}, this is the ambient internal
932	  direct sum \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\), stated entirely inside \(M\) without
933	  requiring a direct-sum decomposition of the subtype \(\smash{\hat p}\).
934	\end{lemma}
935	
936	\begin{proof}
937	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
938	  The inclusion \(\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) \le p\) is clear, since each
939	  \(M_n \cap p \le p\). For the reverse inclusion, take \(m \in p\) and write
940	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) using the
941	  internal decomposition of \(M\). Homogeneity of \(p\) gives
942	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\), hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n \cap p\); thus
943	  \(m\) is a finite sum of elements of the \(M_n \cap p\), i.e.
944	  \(m \in \bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p)\).
945	\end{proof}
946	
947	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient class and its Hilbert function}
948	
949	\begin{definition}\leanok
950	[Subquotient Hilbert data]
951	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
952	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
953	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum}
954	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.hilb}
955	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
956	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
957	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
958	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
959	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
960	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
961	  \begin{itemize}
962	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
963	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
964	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
965	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
966	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
967	    \item a finiteness condition: the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a finite module over the
968	      polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\) (via the
969	      ambient \(t\)-stable carriers \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N\),
970	      \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N'\) of \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}).
971	  \end{itemize}
972	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
973	  Hilbert function} is
974	  \[
975	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
976	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
977	  \]
978	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
979	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
980	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
981	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
982	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
983	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
984	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
985	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
986	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
987	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
988	\end{definition}
989	
990	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
991	
992	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
993	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
994	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
995	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
996	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
997	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
998	
999	\begin{definition}\leanok
1000	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1001	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1002	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1003	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1004	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1005	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1006	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1007	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1008	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1009	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1010	\end{definition}
1011	
1012	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1013	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1014	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1015	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1016	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1017	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1018	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1019	\end{lemma}
1020	
1021	\begin{proof}
1022	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1023	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1024	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1025	\end{proof}
1026	
1027	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1028	[Degree-shift commutation]
1029	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1030	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1031	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1032	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1033	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1034	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1035	  \[
1036	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1037	  \]
1038	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1039	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1040	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1041	\end{lemma}
1042	
1043	\begin{proof}
1044	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1045	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1046	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1047	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1048	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1049	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1050	\end{proof}
1051	
1052	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1053	[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1054	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1055	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1056	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1057	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1058	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1059	\end{lemma}
1060	
1061	\begin{proof}
1062	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1063	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1064	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1065	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1066	\end{proof}
1067	
1068	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1069	[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1070	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1071	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1072	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1073	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1074	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1075	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1076	\end{lemma}
1077	
1078	\begin{proof}
1079	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1080	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1081	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1082	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1083	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1084	  homogeneous.
1085	\end{proof}
1086	
1087	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1088	[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1089	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1090	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1091	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1092	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1093	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1094	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1095	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1096	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1097	\end{lemma}
1098	
1099	\begin{proof}
1100	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1101	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1102	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1103	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1104	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1105	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1106	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1107	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1108	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1109	  homogeneous.
1110	\end{proof}
1111	
1112	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1113	[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1114	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1115	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1116	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1117	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1118	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1119	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1120	  this is recorded here.
1121	\end{lemma}
1122	
1123	\begin{proof}
1124	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1125	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1126	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1127	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1128	\end{proof}
1129	
1130	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1131	[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1132	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1133	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1134	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1135	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1136	\end{lemma}
1137	
1138	\begin{proof}
1139	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1140	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1141	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1142	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1143	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1144	\end{proof}
1145	
1146	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1147	[Ambient image identity]
1148	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1149	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1150	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1151	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1152	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1153	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1154	  \[
1155	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1156	  \]
1157	\end{lemma}
1158	
1159	\begin{proof}
1160	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1161	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1162	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1163	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1164	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1165	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1166	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1167	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1168	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1169	\end{proof}
1170	
1171	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1172	[Ambient distributive law]
1173	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1175	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1176	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1177	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1178	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1179	  \[
1180	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1181	  \]
1182	\end{lemma}
1183	
1184	\begin{proof}
1185	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1186	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1187	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1188	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1189	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1190	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1191	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1192	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1193	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1194	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1195	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1196	\end{proof}
1197	
1198	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1199	
1200	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1201	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1202	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1203	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1204	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1205	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1206	
1207	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1208	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1209	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1211	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1212	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1213	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1214	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1215	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1216	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1217	\end{lemma}
1218	
1219	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1220	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1221	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1222	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1223	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1224	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1225	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1226	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1227	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1228	\end{lemma}
1229	
1230	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1231	[The kernel pair nests]
1232	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1233	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1234	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1235	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1236	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1237	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1238	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1239	\end{lemma}
1240	
1241	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1242	[The cokernel pair nests]
1243	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1244	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1245	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1246	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1247	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1248	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1249	\end{lemma}
1250	
1251	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1252	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1253	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1254	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1255	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1256	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1257	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1258	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1259	\end{lemma}
1260	
1261	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1262	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1263	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1264	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1265	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1266	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1267	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1268	\end{lemma}
1269	
1270	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1271	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1272	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1274	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1275	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1276	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1277	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1278	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1279	\end{lemma}
1280	
1281	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1282	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1283	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1284	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1285	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1286	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1287	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1288	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1289	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1290	\end{lemma}
1291	
1292	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1293	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1294	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1295	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1296	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1297	  % length-(r-1) datum.
1298	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1299	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1300	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1301	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1302	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1303	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1304	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1305	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1306	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1307	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1308	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1309	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1310	  \[
1311	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1312	    \qquad
1313	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1314	  \]
1315	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1316	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1317	  \begin{enumerate}
1318	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1319	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1320	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1321	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1322	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1323	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1324	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1325	  \end{enumerate}
1326	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1327	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1328	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1329	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1330	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1331	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1332	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1333	\end{lemma}
1334	
1335	\begin{proof}
1336	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1337	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1338	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1339	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1340	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1341	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1342	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1343	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1344	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1345	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1346	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1347	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1348	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1349	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1350	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1351	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1352	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1353	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1354	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1355	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1356	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1357	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1358	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1359	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1360	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1361	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1362	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1363	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1364	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1365	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1366	
1367	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1368	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1369	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1370	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1371	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1372	
1373	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1374	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1375	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1376	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1377	
1378	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1379	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1380	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1381	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1382	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1383	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1384	\end{proof}

1042	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
1043	\label{sec:gr_glued}
1044	
1045	\begin{definition}
1046	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1047	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
1048	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
1049	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1050	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
1051	  % affine patches" (read from
1052	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
1053	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1054	  % "Therefore the schemes
1055	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
1056	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
1057	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
1058	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
1059	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
1060	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
1061	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
1062	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1063	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
1064	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
1065	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
1066	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
1067	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
1068	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
1069	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
1070	  \[
1071	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
1072	  \]
1073	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
1074	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
1075	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
1076	
1077	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
1078	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
1079	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
1080	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
1081	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
1082	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
1083	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
1084	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
1085	  follows.
1086	\end{definition}

1088	\section{Separatedness}
1089	\label{sec:gr_separated}
1090	
1091	\begin{lemma}
1092	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
1093	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
1094	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
1095	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1096	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
1097	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
1098	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1099	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
1100	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
1101	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
1102	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1103	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1104	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
1105	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
1106	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
1107	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
1108	\end{lemma}
1109	
1110	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
1111	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
1112	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
1113	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
1114	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
1115	\begin{proof}
1116	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
1117	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
1118	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
1119	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
1120	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
1121	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
1122	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
1123	  homomorphism is surjective.
1124	
1125	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
1126	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
1127	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
1128	  \[
1129	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
1130	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
1131	    \qquad
1132	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1133	  \]
1134	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
1135	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
1136	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
1137	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
1138	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
1139	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
1140	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
1141	  and in particular
1142	  \[
1143	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
1144	  \]
1145	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
1146	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
1147	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
1148	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
1149	  \[
1150	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
1151	  \]
1152	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
1153	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
1154	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
1155	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
1156	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
1157	\end{proof}

1225	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
1226	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
1227	  \[

1240	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
1241	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
1242	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
1243	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
1244	  \(K\). Define
1245	  \[
1246	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
1247	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
1248	  \]
1249	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
1250	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
1251	  landing in \(U^J\).
1252	
1253	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
1254	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
1255	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
1256	  \[
1257	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
1258	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
1259	    \qquad
1260	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
1261	  \]
1262	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
1263	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
1264	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
1265	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
1266	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
1267	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
1268	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
1269	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
1270	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
1271	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
1272	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
1273	
1274	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
1275	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
1276	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1277	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
1278	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
1279	  proper.
1280	\end{proof}
1281	
1282	\section{Out of scope}
1283	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
1284	
1285	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
1286	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
1287	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and
1288	are not treated here:
1289	\begin{itemize}
1290	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
1291	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
1292	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding.
1293	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
1294	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
1295	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
1296	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as
1297	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
1298	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
1299	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
1300	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
1301	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
1302	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
1303	    that base change.
1304	\end{itemize}
1305

1400	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1401	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1402	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1403	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1404	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1405	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1406	  \[
1407	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1408	  \]
1409	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1410	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1411	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1412	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1413	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1414	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1415	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1416	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1417	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1418	\end{lemma}
1419	
1420	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1421	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1422	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1423	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1424	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1425	%  following short exact sequence
1426	%  $$
1427	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1428	%  $$
1429	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1430	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1431	\begin{proof}
1432	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1433	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1434	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1435	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1436	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1437	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1438	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1439	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1440	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1441	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1442	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1443	
1444	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1445	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1446	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1447	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1448	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1449	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1450	
1451	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1452	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1453	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1454	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1455	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1456	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1457	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1458	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1459	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1460	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1461	
1462	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1463	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1464	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1465	  \[
1466	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1467	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1468	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1469	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1470	  \]
1471	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1472	\end{proof}
1473	
1474	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1475	
1476	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1477	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1478	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1479	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1480	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1481	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1482	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1483	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1484	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1485	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1486	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1487	
1488	\begin{definition}\leanok
1489	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1490	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1491	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1492	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1493	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1494	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1495	  ring homomorphism}
1496	  \[
1497	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1498	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1499	  \]
1500	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1501	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1502	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1503	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1504	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1505	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1506	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1507	\end{definition}
1508	
1509	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1510	[Evaluation sends $X_i$ to $t_i$]
1511	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1512	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_X}
1513	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1514	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) for each \(i\). Immediate from the construction
1515	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1516	  \(t_i\).
1517	\end{lemma}
1518	
1519	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1520	[Evaluation sends a constant to a scalar]
1521	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1522	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_C}
1523	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1524	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\) for each \(c \in \kappa\):
1525	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and
1526	  carries the constant polynomial \(C\,c\) to the scalar endomorphism \(c \cdot 1\).
1527	\end{lemma}
1528	
1529	\begin{definition}\leanok
1530	[Polynomial-ring module structure on $M$]
1531	  \label{def:graded_polyModule}
1532	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule}
1533	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1534	  The \emph{polynomial-module structure} is the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module
1535	  structure on \(M\) obtained by restricting scalars along
1536	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}): a polynomial \(p\) acts on
1537	  \(m \in M\) by \(p \cdot m = \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m)\). This is the module over the
1538	  free polynomial ring on which the ambient subquotient induction's finiteness condition
1539	  is measured.
1540	\end{definition}
1541	
1542	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1543	[$X_i$ acts as $t_i$]
1544	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}
1545	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_X_smul}
1546	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1547	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \(X_i \cdot m =
1548	  t_i(m)\) for all \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1549	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}).
1550	\end{lemma}
1551	
1552	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1553	[A constant acts as a scalar]
1554	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1555	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_C_smul}
1556	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1557	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \((C\,c) \cdot m =
1558	  c \cdot m\) for all \(c \in \kappa\), \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1559	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\)
1560	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1561	\end{lemma}
1562	
1563	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1564	[$\kappa$-scalar tower]
1565	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1566	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_isScalarTower}
1567	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1568	  The polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}) is compatible with the
1569	  original \(\kappa\)-action: \(\mathrm{IsScalarTower}\,\kappa\,\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1570	  t_{r-1}]\,M\). Indeed the algebra map \(\kappa \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1571	  followed by the polynomial action recovers the \(\kappa\)-action, since a scalar
1572	  \(c \in \kappa\) maps to the constant \(C\,c\), which acts as \(c\)
1573	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1574	\end{lemma}
1575	
1576	\begin{definition}\leanok
1577	[Polynomial-ring submodule from a $t$-stable submodule]
1578	  \label{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1579	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule}
1580	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul,
1581	    lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1582	  Let \(N \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule that is stable under each \(t_i\)
1583	  (\(t_i \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then \(N\), with the \emph{same} ambient carrier, is a
1584	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule of \(M\) for the polynomial-module structure
1585	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}). Stability under the action of an arbitrary polynomial
1586	  follows by induction on monomials: constants act as scalars
1587	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}) and multiplication by \(X_i\) acts as \(t_i\)
1588	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}), both of which preserve \(N\).
1589	\end{definition}
1590	
1591	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1592	[The polynomial submodule has the same carrier]
1593	  \label{lem:graded_polySubmodule_coe}
1594	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule_coe}
1595	  \uses{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1596	  The underlying set of \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}(t, N)\)
1597	  (\cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}) equals \(N\): the lift to a
1598	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule does not change the carrier, so every object
1599	  in the induction remains an ambient submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1600	\end{lemma}
1601	
1602	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1603	
1604	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness transfer for the subquotients]
1605	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1606	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1607	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1608	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib,
1609	    def:graded_polyEndHom, def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1610	    lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1611	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r \ge 1\)
1612	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), so that \(M\) is finite over
1613	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Then the two
1614	  subquotients
1615	  \[
1616	    K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N'), \qquad C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)
1617	  \]
1618	  of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} are finite modules over
1619	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\); that is, they are subquotient data of length
1620	  \(r-1\), satisfying the finiteness condition. This is what is needed for the
1621	  inductive hypothesis of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}.
1622	
1623	  The kernel \(K\) and cokernel \(C\) of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1624	  satisfy the same conditions as the original datum -- a homogeneous pair, pairwise-commuting
1625	  degree-raising endomorphisms preserving the pair, a polynomial-ring module structure, and
1626	  the finiteness condition -- but of length \(r-1\) rather than \(r\).
1627	\end{lemma}
1628	
1629	\begin{proof}
1630	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1631	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib,
1632	    def:graded_polyEndHom, def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1633	    lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1634	  \emph{The polynomial module structure on \(M\).} The \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1635	  degree-raising endomorphisms \(x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\) generate a \(\kappa\)-subalgebra
1636	  \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\}\) of the \emph{noncommutative}
1637	  endomorphism algebra \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); because the generators
1638	  commute pairwise, \(A\) is commutative (\cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}).
1639	  Commutativity of \(A\) is exactly what makes the evaluation homomorphism at the
1640	  generators a legal \(\kappa\)-algebra homomorphism \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1641	  \to A\): evaluation refuses the noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) as a
1642	  target. Composing with the inclusion \(A \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1643	  and restricting scalars along it endows \(M\) with the structure of a module over
1644	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action and
1645	  characterised by \(t_i \cdot m = x_i m\).
1646	
1647	  \emph{Transfer down one generator.} Set \(A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) and
1648	  \(x = t_{r-1}\). Since \(M\) is finite over the Noetherian ring \(A\), \(M\) is a
1649	  Noetherian \(A\)-module, so the submodule \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq M\) is
1650	  finite over \(A\) and the quotients \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\) and
1651	  \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) are finite over \(A\). By
1652	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} the element \(x = t_{r-1}\) annihilates
1653	  both subquotients, so the \(A\)-action on each factors through the surjection of
1654	  polynomial rings
1655	  \[
1656	    A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]
1657	  \]
1658	  fixing \(t_i\) for \(i < r-1\) and sending \(t_{r-1} \mapsto 0\). Applying the
1659	  finite-generation transfer along a surjection
1660	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}) turns finiteness over \(A\)
1661	  into finiteness over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), supplying the length-\((r-1)\)
1662	  finiteness witnesses for \(K\) and \(C\).
1663	
1664	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1665	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), not the subalgebra
1666	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-2}\} \subseteq
1667	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1668	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection that
1669	  the scalar transfer requires. Keeping free polynomial rings at every step keeps the
1670	  surjection -- and hence the transfer -- available throughout. Everything is finite
1671	  generation of ambient submodules of the fixed \(M\) and their quotients.
1672	\end{proof}
1673	
1674	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
1675	
1676	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1677	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
1678	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
1679	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
1680	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1681	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1682	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
1683	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
1684	  \[
1685	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
1686	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
1687	  \]
1688	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
1689	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
1690	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
1691	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
1692	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
1693	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
1694	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
1695	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
1696	  \[
1697	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
1698	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
1699	  \]
1700	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
1701	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
1702	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
1703	\end{lemma}
1704	
1705	\begin{proof}
1706	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1707	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1708	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
1709	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
1710	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
1711	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
1712	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
1713	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
1714	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
1715	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
1716	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
1717	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
1718	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
1719	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
1720	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
1721	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
1722	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1723	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
1724	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
1725	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
1726	\end{proof}
1727	
1728	\subsubsection*{The base case ($r = 0$)}
1729	
1730	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1731	[Base-case finiteness over the empty polynomial ring]
1732	  \label{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
1733	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
1734	  Let \(Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-vector space that is also a module over
1735	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\) for an \emph{empty} index set
1736	  \(\sigma\) -- in particular \(\sigma = \{0, \dots, r-1\}\) with \(r = 0\) -- in a way
1737	  compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action (scalar tower). If \(Q\) is module-finite over
1738	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\), then \(Q\) is finite-dimensional over
1739	  \(\kappa\). Indeed for empty \(\sigma\) the polynomial ring is
1740	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), a finite \(\kappa\)-module,
1741	  so finiteness of \(Q\) over it transfers to finiteness over \(\kappa\) along the scalar
1742	  tower. This is the length-zero input to the ambient subquotient induction
1743	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}): when \(r = 0\) the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a
1744	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space, hence \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero.
1745	\end{lemma}
1746	
1747	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}
1748	
1749	\begin{lemma}[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
1750	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
1751	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
1752	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1753	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1754	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1755	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
1756	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
1757	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1758	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1759	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1760	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
1761	  degree-one generators).}
1762	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
1763	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
1764	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
1765	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1766	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
1767	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
1768	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
1769	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
1770	  \(M\).
1771	\end{lemma}
1772	
1773	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
1774	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1775	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
1776	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
1777	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
1778	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
1779	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
1780	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
1781	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
1782	\begin{proof}
1783	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1784	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1785	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1786	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
1787	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
1788	  We induct on the length \(r\).
1789	
1790	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} With no endomorphisms, the finiteness witness says
1791	  \(M\) is a finite module over \(\kappa[\,] = \kappa\), so \(M\) is a
1792	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space. Hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) and
1793	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) for all \(n \gg 0\), so \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for
1794	  \(n \gg 0\). By \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is
1795	  rational of order \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
1796	
1797	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
1798	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
1799	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
1800	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), with valid
1801	  finiteness witnesses supplied by the finiteness transfer
1802	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The induction hypothesis applied
1803	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
1804	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
1805	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1806	  \[
1807	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
1808	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
1809	  \]
1810	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
1811	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
1812	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
1813	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
1814	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
1815	\end{proof}
